early universe
today
galaxies can fade — spacetime curvature remains

visible galaxylight traces part of the halo

light collapses inwardthe halo is still there

gas hides the lightgravity remains measurable

halo before starsstructure can form before galaxies light up

merger scrambles lightthe mass map is cleaner

light changes; gravity gives the census
count the gravity wells, not only the light
initial parameters
primordial seeds
matter P(k)
smoothing W(kR)
σ(M)
collapse threshold
halo counts
before the graph

P(k) is a size-by-size map of structure.

small k huge cosmic region mostly smooth after averaging
k ↑ means size ↓
large k small detailed region many sharp clumps
P(k) = how strong the density fluctuations are at that k
matter power spectrum

P(k) tells us which universe sizes received the most structure.

large scales / small ksmall scales / large k
01P(k)scale map
02W(kR)smoothing
03δ > δccollapse
04dn/dlnMhalo counts

Now we choose a mass scale and smooth the dark matter field.

same radius windows sample different parts of the universe
window function
top-hat windowaverage everything inside R
δR+0.42
σ at one mass scale

Every smoothing sphere becomes one density measurement.

σ = spread of smoothed densities
collapse threshold

Only the rare overdense tail crosses δc and becomes halos.

δc ≈ 1.686
01P(k)scale map
02W(kR)smoothing
03δ > δccollapse
04dn/dlnMhalo counts
final dive

Repeat this across mass scales, then count the collapsed objects.

small halos are common; massive halos are rare
change the smoothing mass

Bigger regions average more universe, so σ(M) falls with mass.

slope → d ln σ / d ln M
halo mass function

The universe makes many small halos and very few giant ones.

final HMF graph: abundance drops steeply with halo mass
takeaway

Halo counts turn invisible gravity into a testable history of the universe.
